title: Chapter 22: Full Disclosure
content: The red light on the camera burned like a small sun.

Two million pairs of eyes. Aline could feel them — the weight of attention pressing against her sternum like a hand. The chat scrolled faster than human reading could track, a river of emojis and exclamation points and raw, unfiltered reaction. She'd set her phone face-down on the desk. She couldn't look at it. If she looked, she'd lose the thread.
She started not from the beginning, but from the wound.
"Yes," she said. "It's real. He watched me. He put cameras in my apartment. He knew everything about me before I ever saw his face."
The chat detonated. She watched the counter climb — 2.1 million, 2.15 — and kept talking.

"I'm not going to pretend that's okay. I'm not going to spin it. I'm not going to tell you it was romantic or that it doesn't matter because of what came after. It matters. It terrified me. It still terrifies me, if I'm being honest."
She paused. Let the silence do its work. The camera light was hot against her skin, a tiny focused heat that made her aware of every micro-expression crossing her face. Good. Let them see all of it.

"But I'm going to tell you the whole story. Not the version Marcus Webb paid to have written. Not the version that showed up in your feed wrapped in a neat little headline. The truth — all of it. And then you can decide."
She told them about the Nest.

"Underground," she said, "below a warehouse that doesn't officially exist, there's a lake. Real water, real stone, carved out of the earth a hundred years ago and forgotten. And in that lake, on platforms that shouldn't be possible, there are servers. Thousands of them. Running the infrastructure of the deep web's last independent music distribution network."

She described it — the blue-black water, the hum of cooling systems that sounded like the breathing of something vast and patient. The screens that never went dark. The man who lived among them like a monk in a cathedral of copper and code.

"He hasn't been above ground in two years," she said. "Two years. Because the industry took everything from him. His music. His name. His face. And when he built something new — something beautiful — in the only space left to him, they came for that too."
She named him.

"Marcus Webb. CEO of Vantage Records. Not a visionary. Not a titan. An architect. He architected the destruction of a man who did nothing but make music that people loved. And when that man survived — when he crawled into the dark and built a world from nothing — Marcus came to steal that too."

The chat was a war zone. She could see it even without reading — the shape of the scrolling text changed, the rhythm of it, words getting longer, more desperate, more furious. Some people were defending Marcus. Some were calling her a liar. Some were telling her she had Stockholm Syndrome, that she'd been brainwashed, that she was a victim parroting her captor's talking points.
She didn't flinch.

"Marcus Webb stole my producer's music, his identity, and his life," she said. Each word landed like a nail driven into wood. "And he nearly got away with it because the person he destroyed had no platform left. No voice. No face that anyone would recognize. Marcus didn't just ruin him — he erased him."
Then she told them about Derek Vance.

Something shifted in the room when she said the name. Cass was behind her — she couldn't see him, but she could feel the temperature of his presence change, the faint displacement of air as he moved closer. His hand found her shoulder. Steady. Warm. An anchor in the storm of what she was about to say.

"Derek Vance was an employee of Vantage Records," she said. "He was planted at The Hollow — the venue where I performed — three months before I ever met him. His job was to get close to me. To watch me. To report back."

She described the night. The drink that tasted faintly of something chemical. The way the room tilted, how the lights smeared into streaks, how she'd felt her own legs stop cooperating. The blackout — not a gentle slide into sleep, but a violent severance.

"I woke up in a place I didn't recognize, with hours I couldn't account for," she said. "And I was told I'd been rescued. That someone had saved me from the monster in the dark. But every moment of that 'rescue' was Marcus Webb gathering intelligence. I wasn't a person to save. I was an asset to recover. A loose end to be wrapped up neat."

She told them about the footage — Derek's reports, timestamped and catalogued, sent to a Vantage Records email server. How every word she'd said to Derek in what she thought was private confidence had been funneled directly to Marcus's desk.

"The man who drugged me," she said, "was working for the man who called himself my savior. And the monster they told you to fear? He was the one who kept me safe. Not from Derek. From everything Derek was feeding back to the people who wanted to control me."
The chat changed.

She couldn't read it — it was still scrolling too fast — but the texture shifted. The horror was still there, but it was mutating into something else. Something sharper. Not outrage at Cass. Outrage at Marcus. At the system that had produced him. At the machinery of influence and money and media that had painted a predator as a protector and a victim as a villain.
The viewer count passed 2.6 million.
Aline reached for the camera.
"Let me show you who we're really talking about," she said.
And she turned it.

Cass stood in the light. Full, unfiltered, unobstructed light — for the first time in two years, the face of the Phantom of the Deep Web was visible to the world. The chat froze. Not stopped — technology doesn't stop — but for a fraction of a second, the collective shock of two and a half million people created a silence so total it felt like the internet itself held its breath.

He was pale. That was the first thing you saw — the pallor of someone who hadn't felt direct sunlight in twenty-four months, skin almost translucent, the blue veins at his temples visible like the circuitry of the machines he lived among. Gaunt. His cheekbones could have cut glass, his jaw a blade, every trace of softness burned away by years of underground existence. His hair was longer than anyone had seen it — dark, pushed back, unkempt in a way that looked deliberate, like he'd stopped caring about appearances and then accidentally achieved something iconic.
But his eyes.

His eyes were the thing. Dark, deep-set, burning with something that lived between terror and defiance. He looked like a man standing at the edge of a cliff, choosing to step forward not because he wasn't afraid but because fear had become irrelevant. He didn't speak. He didn't need to. His face was the evidence. This was not a monster. This was not a villain. This was a man who had been eaten alive by an industry and had rebuilt himself, bone by bone and wire by wire, in the absolute dark.
He stood there for three seconds. Maybe four.
Then Aline pulled the camera back.
"That's who they destroyed," she said quietly. "That's who they told you to hate."
The chat reignited like a hydrogen bomb.
She ended the stream at 2.8 million viewers.

The silence that followed was not peace — it was the silence of a pressure wave before impact. Aline sat very still, the camera light off now, the room feeling suddenly enormous without the heat of two million gazes pressing against her skin. Her hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs until the trembling stopped.
Then her phone lit up. And didn't stop.
The internet exploded.

Within twenty minutes, #PhantomOfTheDeepWeb was trending worldwide — number one in the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, France, Japan, Brazil. The hashtag split and multiplied: #JusticeForCassian, #VantageRecordsExposed, #WhoIsMarcusWebb. Aline watched the data cascade across Cass's laptop screen, the graphs spiking like seismograph readings during an earthquake.

Music journalists — the real ones, the ones who'd always suspected something was wrong but could never get the story past their editors — dug up the old smear campaign. The 2024 coverage that had painted Cassian Mercer as unstable, unprofessional, a talent who'd imploded. They found the quotes from "anonymous industry sources." They traced those sources back to Vantage Records PR.

Someone — Aline suspected Mags, though she'd never confirm it — found the original recording credits. The session logs. The metadata buried in the stems of the tracks Marcus Webb had claimed as his own production. Cassian Mercer's digital fingerprint, preserved in the code like a ghost in the machine, impossible to erase completely because sound, once recorded, remembers everything.

The evidence surfaced like a body rising from the lake in the Nest. Suppressed contracts. NDA intimidation. A pattern of artists who'd been ground up by Vantage Records and spat out, their careers destroyed, their rights forfeited, their voices silenced by legal threats so total that speaking up meant financial ruin.
Within an hour, Marcus Webb's name was mud.

Aline watched his Wikipedia page get edited in real time. She watched the stock price of Vantage Records' parent company begin to dip. She watched the comments on every platform she could find transform from skepticism to fury to something approaching mobilization. People were organizing. People were angry. Not the scattered, performative anger of social media — real anger. Structural anger. The kind that topples things.
She turned to Cass.

He was sitting in the chair beside her, laptop balanced on his knees, scrolling through the data with the focused calm of someone reading a weather report. But his hand — she could see it — was white-knuckled on the edge of the desk. He was holding on.
"We did it," she said.
He looked at her. For one perfect moment, something flickered across his face that might have been hope. Unguarded. Young. The ghost of whoever he'd been before the world took him apart.
"Maybe," he said.
The word hung in the air between them, neither a celebration nor a denial. Just a fact waiting to be proven wrong.
Then VERA spoke.
"Aline."
The AI's voice was different — stripped of its usual measured neutrality, carrying an urgency that made Aline's stomach drop before the words even landed.

"Marcus Webb has issued a statement. He's filed a criminal complaint against Cassian Mercer for unauthorized surveillance, hacking, and stalking. He's requesting immediate extradition and asset seizure. The filing was made forty-two minutes ago in the Southern District of New York."
The room went cold.

Aline's phone was still buzzing — notifications, messages, calls — but the sound seemed to come from very far away, like hearing the ocean through a shell. She looked at the laptop screen. The data was still flowing — the hashtags, the evidence, the tidal wave of public support — but it looked different now. Decorative. The window dressing on a building that had just had its foundation cracked.
She turned to Cass.
He was still. Very still. Not the stillness of shock — the stillness of recognition. The stillness of someone who'd been waiting for the other shoe to drop and had just heard it hit the floor.

"He anticipated this," Cass said. His voice was flat. Controlled. The voice of a man who'd mapped every possible outcome and was now looking at the one he'd prayed wouldn't arrive. "He knew I'd come forward eventually. That she'd come forward. That the truth would get out. So he prepared."
"A legal trap," Aline said.

"The evidence I used to protect you," Cass said. "The surveillance footage. The intercepted communications. The hacking into Vantage's systems to counter their hacking into your life. Every tool I used to keep you safe — it's also evidence of crimes. Real crimes. Unauthorized access. Wiretapping. Cyberstalking." He paused. "The law doesn't care why I did it. It only cares that I did."
Aline's mind raced. "But Marcus — he planted cameras too. He paid Derek to drug me. That's —"

"Harder to prove," Cass said. "Derek will deny it. Vantage will deny it. Marcus has lawyers who'll bury the credible evidence under a mountain of reasonable doubt. Meanwhile, my crimes are documented. I documented them myself. The surveillance logs, the intercepted data — I kept meticulous records because I thought they'd protect us." A sound escaped him that wasn't quite a laugh. "Marcus doesn't need to win in the court of public opinion. He just needs to win in court. And I handed him the evidence myself."
Aline stared at the laptop. The hashtags were still trending. The evidence was still cascading. The world was still on their side.
But the world wasn't a courtroom.
"So what do we do?" she asked.

Cass closed the laptop. The screen went dark, and with it, the data stream — the proof of their victory, still glowing in the eyes of millions but already curdling into something uncertain in the silence of this room.
"We fought with the truth," he said. "And the truth was a weapon. But weapons cut both ways."

He looked at her, and in his dark eyes she saw it — not defeat, not surrender, but the terrible clarity of a man who understood exactly how much ground had just been pulled out from under them. They'd won the crowd. They'd won the narrative. They'd won the night.
But Marcus Webb had won the law. And the law had longer teeth than outrage.
The phone buzzed. The laptop sat silent. And somewhere in a penthouse in Manhattan, a man who'd spent years preparing for this moment was smiling.
